3.happening: We start with result as an empty list.
3.why: The brute force search will fill this only after it finds two indices whose values add to target.
3.invariant: No pair has been accepted yet.

4.happening: Index i moves to the next possible first number in the pair.
4.why: Fixing one index lets us compare it with every later index.
4.invariant: All pairs that start before i have already been checked and did not need to be kept.

5.happening: We read nums[i], so first becomes {nums[i]}.
5.why: Naming the first value makes the upcoming pair sum clear.
5.invariant: i is fixed while j searches to the right.

6.happening: Index j starts or advances to a position after i.
6.why: Starting at i + 1 prevents using the same element twice and avoids repeating pairs in reverse order.
6.invariant: Every checked pair has two different indices.

7.happening: We read nums[j], so second becomes {nums[j]}.
7.why: This gives us the second value for the current candidate pair.
7.invariant: The current pair is exactly the two highlighted indices i and j.

8.happening: We compute current_sum as first + second.
8.why: The only test for a valid pair is whether this sum equals target.
8.invariant: No answer is returned until a pair's sum is proven equal to target.

9.happening: current_sum ({current_sum}) equals target ({target}), so the highlighted pair is the answer.
9.why: The problem asks for indices of two numbers whose values add to target.
9.invariant: The valid answer uses two distinct indices: i and j.

9.happening: current_sum ({current_sum}) does not equal target ({target}), so this pair is rejected.
9.why: A pair is useful only if its sum exactly matches target.
9.invariant: The search continues without changing any previously checked pair.

10.happening: We store the answer indices as result = [i, j].
10.why: These are the positions of the two values that add to target.
10.invariant: result now contains a valid two-index answer.

11.why: The problem guarantees exactly one valid answer, so there is no need to search after finding it.
11.invariant: The returned indices point to two different numbers whose sum is target.

init.why: The algorithm needs a place to store the matching indices.
init.invariant: No candidate pair has been accepted.

loop.why: Trying every pair guarantees the valid one will be found.
loop.invariant: The algorithm only compares pairs with two distinct indices.

update.happening: Read values or update the stored result.
update.why: Each comparison needs the values at the highlighted indices.
update.invariant: The current state describes one concrete candidate pair.

check.happening: Compare the current pair's sum with target.
check.why: A pair is correct exactly when its sum equals target.

return.why: A valid pair has been found.
return.invariant: The returned result satisfies nums[result[0]] + nums[result[1]] == target.
